SSM Updated Simulations — Detailed Report

10 locations · 4 genotypes (Non-LT, LT1.5, LT2, LT2.5) · 6 managements · 1990–2019 (30 years)

Generated: 2026-05-29 · Data: modeling_data/data/updated-outputs/

Experiment Design

The Sinclair Simplified Soybean Model (SSM) was used to simulate soybean growth and water use across 10 locations in three US states (Arkansas, Missouri, and Nebraska) for the 30-year period 1990–2019. Each simulation represents one combination of location, genotype, water management, and planting date, run for each year independently.

Locations

Locations were selected to span the major US soybean production belt, from the humid Mid-South to the semi-arid Great Plains:

StateLocationsClimate
ArkansasJonesboro, Keiser, Marianna, RohwerHumid subtropical; high VPD summers; drought risk during reproductive stages
MissouriAlbany, Mount Vernon, NoveltyHumid continental; moderate summer VPD; variable rainfall
NebraskaEustis, Lincoln, North PlatteSemi-arid to sub-humid; high summer VPD; irrigation-dependent production

Genotypes and the LT Trait

Four genotype classes were simulated using maturity groups matched to each region: MG 4 for Arkansas and Missouri, and MG 3 for Nebraska — chosen to approximate locally adapted commercial varieties and keep phenology comparable within each region:

Genotype classSSM crop nameMG (AR & MO / NE)LT trait descriptionVPD threshold
Non-LTSOYBEAN-MG4 / SOYBEAN-MG3MG4 (AR, MO) / MG3 (NE)Standard cultivar — transpiration increases linearly with VPD; no stomatal limitNone
LT1.5SOYBEAN-LT1.5-MG4/3MG4 (AR, MO) / MG3 (NE)Mild limited transpiration — stomata restrict TR above ~1.5 kPa VPD~1.5 kPa
LT2SOYBEAN-LT2-MG4/3MG4 (AR, MO) / MG3 (NE)Moderate LT — TR limited above ~2.0 kPa (canonical PI 416937 threshold)~2.0 kPa
LT2.5SOYBEAN-LT2.5-MG4/3MG4 (AR, MO) / MG3 (NE)Strong LT — TR limited above ~2.5 kPa; most conservative water use~2.5 kPa

The LT trait is encoded in the SSM by a VPD-threshold response: when daily VPD exceeds the genotype-specific threshold, transpiration is capped at the rate reached at that VPD level, conserving soil water. Above-threshold days are common in all three states during July–August, making the LT trait agronomically relevant across the simulation domain.

Water Management

Each genotype was simulated under two water management regimes:

  • Rainfed (RFD) — no supplemental water; crop relies entirely on rainfall and stored soil water. This represents the majority of US soybean production.
  • Irrigated (IRRI) — water is supplied on demand to keep the fraction of transpirable soil water (FTSW) above stress thresholds. This isolates the atmospheric demand effect (VPD) from soil water limitation, providing a cleaner signal of the LT trait's transpiration response.

Planting Dates

Three planting dates were simulated per location to capture agronomic flexibility: Early (ELY), Mid (MID), and Late (LTE). Results reported here are averaged across planting dates unless stated otherwise.

Output Variables

VariableDescriptionUnit
SUMETTSeasonal total crop evapotranspiration (ETc)mm season⁻¹
Yield_kghaGrain yield (WGRN × 10)kg ha⁻¹
WPWater productivity (Yield / SUMETT)kg mm⁻¹
R7.PMDays from planting to physiological maturitydays
ETc (daily)Daily crop ET = TR (transpiration) + SEVP (soil evaporation)mm day⁻¹
LAILeaf area indexm² m⁻²
DDMPDaily dry matter productiong m⁻² day⁻¹

Note: reference ET (ETo) used for Kc calculations is the Penman-Monteith ETo from NASA POWER, not the SSM internal PET output. Seasonal Kc results are reported in the yearly analysis section.

Overview: all locations

Rainfed grain yield by location and genotype

Seasonal ETc (SUMETT) under irrigation by location and genotype

Rainfed yield advantage of LT genotypes vs Non-LT (%)

Irrigation water saving (SUMETT reduction) of LT vs Non-LT

Water productivity (yield / ETc) by water management and genotype

Season length (days to R7.PM) under irrigation

Average Daily Crop ET (ETc) Analysis

Daily ETc = transpiration (TR) + soil evaporation (SEVP), using growing-season days only (DAP > 0). All values are based on 1,337,585 valid daily rows from the 10-location SSM simulation (1990–2019).

D1 — Mean daily ETc by location, management, and genotype

Bars show the grand mean ± SE of all growing-season daily ETc values pooled across years and planting dates for each location × genotype × management combination.

D2 — Seasonal progression of daily ETc (5-day DAP bins)

ETc averaged within consecutive 5-day bins from planting to physiological maturity (full cycle), split by state and management. The thick dashed blue line is the mean daily PM ETo from NASA POWER (FAO-56 Penman-Monteith, state-level representative sites; averaged across 1990–2019 by DAP bin). Dotted grey lines mark approximate vegetative→reproductive transition (~DAP 50) and early reproductive→grain fill (~DAP 90). Irrigated and rainfed panels share the same y-axis. Season length varies by region: up to ~200 DAP in Arkansas, ~240 DAP in Missouri and Nebraska.

D3 — Seasonal progression of Leaf Area Index (LAI)

Mean LAI (m² m⁻²) within 5-day DAP bins by state, management, and genotype. LAI trajectories reflect canopy development and senescence; differences among LT classes indicate the indirect effect of water conservation on leaf area maintenance during reproductive stages.

D4 — Seasonal progression of daily dry matter production (DDMP)

Mean daily dry matter production (g m⁻² day⁻¹) within 5-day DAP bins by state, management, and genotype. DDMP integrates radiation interception and radiation use efficiency; comparing LT and Non-LT reveals whether water conservation translates into maintained or improved carbon assimilation.

D5 — Daily ETc reduction of LT genotypes relative to Non-LT

Positive values indicate water saved per day relative to Non-LT. LT2 and LT2.5 show the largest daily savings under irrigation in all three states.

D6 — Mean daily ETc summary table

Complete numerical summary by location, management, and genotype. Reduction column reports the absolute daily water saving relative to Non-LT (mm day⁻¹).
